topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the office was the loudest thing I’d ever heard. Dust motes danced in the shafts of afternoon sun slicing through the blinds, illuminating the empty chair where Mr. Albright used to sit. I stood by the mahogany desk, my knuckles white where I gripped the edge. He hadn't been in for three days, and no one had mentioned his departure. I felt a slow, burning pressure building behind my eyes, a tightness in my chest that made each breath shallow. When Sarah finally shuffled in, her voice a soft inquiry, I didn't look up. "He's gone," I stated, the words clipped and flat. I didn't bother to offer a greeting, just turned my back on the space he had occupied.

[story 2]

I walked into the study, expecting the familiar scent of aged paper and pipe tobacco, but the air was sterile, unnervingly still. The framed photographs on the mantelpiece seemed to mock me with their cheerful, frozen smiles. My jaw clenched so hard I could feel the muscles vibrating beneath my skin. I circled the room twice, my footsteps echoing too loudly on the polished floor. There was no note, no farewell card tucked into a book. Just an abrupt vacuum where guidance used to reside. A sharp, unpleasant twist settled in my stomach, making me want to slam the door shut and never look back at this quiet monument to absence.

[story 3]

"So, he just vanished?" I asked, the question tasting like ash in my mouth. My voice was dangerously level, betraying the tremor running through my hands. I paced the perimeter of the large, empty workshop, running a hand over the cool metal of a workbench. Every tool, every unfinished project, felt suddenly irrelevant, a testament to a sudden, unceremonious end. I stared at the closed door, my gaze intense and unyielding. A profound sense of being dismissed washed over me, heavy and suffocating, and I felt a distinct urge to shatter something nearby just to release the pressure building inside my skull.
